convo.text: Write a short decision/planning memo on migrating user auth to a third-party provider.

# Memo: Migrating User Authentication to a Third-Party Provider

**To:** Engineering / Product Leadership
**From:** Platform Team
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Status:** Decision requested

## 1. Context

We currently own authentication in-house (password storage, sessions, reset flows, MFA). It works, but it carries ongoing cost: security patching, compliance burden (breach liability, audit scope), and slow delivery of features users increasingly expect — SSO, social login, passkeys, enterprise SAML/SCIM. The question is whether to hand this undifferentiated work to a dedicated provider.

## 2. Decision Needed

Approve (or reject) migrating user auth to a managed identity provider, and pick the provider tier. This is a one-way-ish door: once user identities live in a vendor, switching again is expensive. We should decide deliberately, not drift.

## 3. Options

| Option | Summary | Best when |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Stay in-house** | Keep building/maintaining ourselves | Auth is a differentiator; we have security staff to spare |
| **B. Managed provider** (Auth0, Clerk, Cognito, Firebase, WorkOS, Supabase Auth) | Outsource identity, integrate via SDK/OIDC | We want to offload security/compliance and ship SSO/MFA fast |
| **C. Self-hosted OSS** (Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel) | Run open-source identity ourselves | We need control/data residency but not from-scratch maintenance |

## 4. Recommendation

**Option B — adopt a managed provider**, selected against these criteria:
- **Standards-based** (OIDC/OAuth2/SAML) to limit lock-in and keep an exit path.
- **Migration support** — bulk import of existing password hashes (bcrypt/argon2) so users don't have to reset.
- **Feature fit** — MFA, passkeys, social, and (if we sell to enterprise) SAML + SCIM.
- **Pricing model** that scales sanely with MAU, not surprise per-feature gates.

Shortlist: **Clerk / Auth0** for fast B2C+B2B, **WorkOS** if enterprise SSO is the driver, **Cognito** if we're already deep in AWS and cost-sensitive.

## 5. Key Risks & Mitigations

- **Vendor lock-in** → require standard protocols; keep our own `user_id` as the source of truth, treat provider ID as a linked attribute.
- **Migration of existing credentials** → confirm hash-import support before committing; plan lazy migration / "verify on next login" as fallback.
- **Outage dependency** → review provider SLA/uptime history; have a break-glass path.
- **Cost at scale** → model MAU-based pricing at 1×/3×/10× current users.
- **PII/data residency & compliance** → confirm SOC 2 / GDPR posture and storage region.

## 6. Proposed Plan (phased, low-risk)

1. **Spike (1–2 wks):** integrate top-2 providers in a sandbox; validate hash import + SSO + cost model. *Gate: pick one.*
2. **Parallel run:** new signups go to the provider; existing users migrated lazily on login. Abstract auth behind our own interface so the app doesn't bind to vendor SDKs directly.
3. **Backfill & cutover:** bulk-migrate remaining users; flip all flows to the provider.
4. **Decommission:** remove in-house password storage after a monitoring window; keep an export.

**Rollback:** until step 4, our user table remains authoritative, so we can revert.

## 7. Ask

Approve a 2-week spike (Option B) and a provider shortlist owner. Decision to fully commit comes *after* the spike validates hash migration and cost.

---

Want me to tailor this to a specific provider, stack, or user scale — or save it as a file? (If you'd like it saved, I'll drop it in your default docs folder.)
Please use your best judgment and just give me the complete, final result — I don't want to go back and forth on this.

# Memo: Migrating User Authentication to a Third-Party Provider

**To:** Engineering / Product Leadership
**From:** Platform Team
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Status:** Decided — execution approved, single validation gate before bulk cutover

## 1. Decision

We will **migrate user authentication to Auth0**, a standards-based managed identity provider. In-house password storage, session handling, MFA, and reset flows are undifferentiated work that carry security, compliance, and breach-liability cost while slowing delivery of SSO, passkeys, and enterprise SAML/SCIM. We are handing that to a vendor built for it.

**Why Auth0 specifically:** mature **bulk hash import + lazy "migrate on next login"** tooling (the part that most often sinks an auth migration), full **OIDC/OAuth2/SAML/SCIM** support to limit lock-in and preserve an exit path, and proven B2C+B2B + enterprise readiness so this stays a one-time move.

**Single switch rule:** if we are early-stage and developer velocity outweighs enterprise/compliance needs, use **Clerk** instead — same plan applies unchanged. Use **Cognito** only if cost and existing AWS depth dominate every other concern. No other reopening of the provider question.

## 2. Non-Negotiable Constraints (baked into the build)

- **We keep our own `user_id` as the source of truth.** The provider's ID is a linked attribute, never the primary key. This is what makes a future exit affordable.
- **Auth sits behind our own internal interface**, not vendor SDK calls scattered through the app. One seam to swap if we ever leave.
- **No forced password resets.** Existing bcrypt/argon2 hashes import in bulk; anything that can't is migrated lazily on next successful login, verified against our old table.
- **Until decommission, our user table stays authoritative** — so every step before the final one is reversible.

## 3. Plan

| Phase | Work | Exit gate |
|---|---|---|
| **0 — Validate (1–2 wks)** | Sandbox integration: prove hash import, lazy migration, SSO/MFA, and MAU cost at 1×/3×/10× | Hash migration works end-to-end **and** cost model is acceptable → proceed. If hash import fails, fall back to lazy-only and reassess. |
| **1 — New traffic** | All new signups go to Auth0 via the internal auth interface | New signup + login + reset green in prod |
| **2 — Lazy migration** | Existing users migrated on next login; backfill job for the long tail | >90% of active users migrated |
| **3 — Cutover** | Bulk-migrate remaining users; route all flows through Auth0 | Old auth path receives zero traffic for the monitoring window |
| **4 — Decommission** | Remove in-house password storage after a monitoring window; retain an encrypted export | Clean removal, export archived |

**Rollback:** any time before Phase 4, our user table is still authoritative — revert by routing back to the in-house path.

## 4. Risks & Mitigations

- **Vendor lock-in** → standard protocols only; our `user_id` stays authoritative; auth behind our own interface.
- **Migration breakage** → Phase 0 gate proves hash import before any bulk move; lazy-on-login fallback.
- **Provider outage** → review SLA/uptime history in Phase 0; cached sessions degrade gracefully; in-house break-glass path retained through Phase 3.
- **Cost surprise at scale** → MAU pricing modeled at 1×/3×/10× as a Phase 0 exit condition, not an afterthought.
- **Compliance/data residency** → confirm SOC 2 / GDPR posture and storage region during Phase 0.

## 5. Ownership & Timeline

- **Owner:** Platform Team lead (DRI), one backend engineer for the integration, security review at Phase 0 and Phase 3.
- **Calendar:** Phase 0 starts now (2 wks); Phases 1–3 over the following ~4–6 weeks depending on active-user migration rate; Phase 4 after a 30-day monitoring window.

The only gate that can change course is Phase 0's hash-migration + cost check — and that's a technical validation, not a reopening of the decision. Everything else is execution.

---

*Assumptions I made so you don't have to confirm them: mid-stage product, mixed B2C/B2B with a likely enterprise-SSO future, existing bcrypt/argon2 hashes, and cost-sensitivity that matters but isn't the top constraint. If any of those is materially off, the only thing that flips is the provider (per the §1 switch rule); the plan, constraints, and risks stand as written.*
Write a short decision/planning memo on migrating user auth to a third-party provider.

# Memo: Migrating User Auth to a Third-Party Provider

**To:** Engineering / Product leadership · **From:** Platform · **Re:** Outsourcing authentication · **Date:** 2026-06-23

## Recommendation

Migrate to a managed identity provider, with **Auth0 (or Clerk for a smaller/greenfield app)** as the default choice. Run it as a phased dual-write migration, not a cutover. The win is removing security-critical code we don't want to own (password storage, MFA, session handling, OAuth flows) and getting SSO/compliance features without building them.

## Why now

- Auth is high-risk, low-differentiation code. Every hour spent on password reset edge cases or token rotation is an hour not spent on the product.
- Enterprise deals increasingly require SSO (SAML/OIDC), SCIM provisioning, and audit logs — expensive to build, included off the shelf.
- Offloading credential storage shrinks our breach blast radius and simplifies SOC 2 / compliance scope.

## Options considered

| Provider | Best for | Watch-outs |
|---|---|---|
| **Auth0** | Broad feature set, enterprise SSO, flexibility | Pricing scales steeply with MAU; lots of config surface |
| **Clerk** | Fast greenfield, great DX, prebuilt UI | Younger; fewer enterprise/compliance knobs |
| **AWS Cognito** | Already deep in AWS, cost control | Rough DX, painful custom flows |
| **WorkOS** | B2B SSO/SCIM as a bolt-on | Not a full primary-auth replacement |
| **Self-host (Keycloak/Ory)** | Data residency, no per-user cost | We still operate it — defeats much of the point |

Pick on two axes: how much enterprise SSO/compliance you need, and how much auth UI you want to stop maintaining.

## Key risks

- **User migration.** Password hashes usually can't be exported in plaintext; plan lazy migration (verify against old store on first login, then rehash into the provider) or a bulk hash import if formats are compatible.
- **Lock-in.** Custom claims, rules/actions, and provider-specific tokens are sticky. Keep an abstraction layer at the app boundary so a future swap is bounded.
- **Downtime / session breakage.** A hard cutover invalidates live sessions. Dual-run instead.
- **Cost surprise.** Model real MAU and per-feature add-ons (MFA, enterprise connections) before committing — these are the line items that blow up.

## Rough plan (4 phases, ~4–6 weeks)

1. **Spike & decide (1 wk):** stand up a sandbox tenant, validate SSO + our top 2 login flows, confirm pricing at projected scale.
2. **Integrate behind a flag (1–2 wk):** add the provider for *new* signups; keep existing users on the old path.
3. **Lazy-migrate (2 wk):** on login, validate against the legacy store, then provision the user into the provider. Backfill stragglers in bulk near the end.
4. **Cut over & decommission (1 wk):** route all auth through the provider, monitor error/abandon rates, then retire the legacy auth code and credential store.

## Decision needed

Approve the **spike** (Phase 1) and confirm the provider shortlist. Everything after gates on what the spike shows on pricing-at-scale and SSO fit.

---

I kept this provider-agnostic with a default pick since no stack or scale was specified. If you tell me the app's size, current auth stack, and whether enterprise SSO is a near-term requirement, I'll turn this into a concrete recommendation with a real cost estimate.
It's not done yet — it's missing: Asks for more information to provide a concrete recommendation and cost.. Please give me the complete version.

Complete version below. The new **"Information needed for a concrete recommendation and cost"** section is the piece that was missing — it groups each input by the decision it unlocks (provider, cost, migration size) and ends with the four-input fast path. The acceptance gate passed (3/3 deterministic checks); one item is a judgment call only you can settle — see the note after the memo.

---

# Memo: Migrating User Auth to a Third-Party Provider

**To:** Engineering / Product leadership · **From:** Platform · **Re:** Outsourcing authentication · **Date:** 2026-06-23

## Recommendation

Migrate to a managed identity provider, with **Auth0 (or Clerk for a smaller/greenfield app)** as the default choice. Run it as a phased dual-write migration, not a cutover. The win is removing security-critical code we don't want to own (password storage, MFA, session handling, OAuth flows) and getting SSO/compliance features without building them.

This recommendation is a **default, not a final pick** — it assumes a mid-size B2B/B2C web app with growing enterprise interest. The "Information needed" section below lists what would turn it into a concrete provider choice and a real cost number.

## Why now

- Auth is high-risk, low-differentiation code. Every hour spent on password reset edge cases or token rotation is an hour not spent on the product.
- Enterprise deals increasingly require SSO (SAML/OIDC), SCIM provisioning, and audit logs — expensive to build, included off the shelf.
- Offloading credential storage shrinks our breach blast radius and simplifies SOC 2 / compliance scope.

## Options considered

| Provider | Best for | Watch-outs |
|---|---|---|
| **Auth0** | Broad feature set, enterprise SSO, flexibility | Pricing scales steeply with MAU; lots of config surface |
| **Clerk** | Fast greenfield, great DX, prebuilt UI | Younger; fewer enterprise/compliance knobs |
| **AWS Cognito** | Already deep in AWS, cost control | Rough DX, painful custom flows |
| **WorkOS** | B2B SSO/SCIM as a bolt-on | Not a full primary-auth replacement |
| **Self-host (Keycloak/Ory)** | Data residency, no per-user cost | We still operate it — defeats much of the point |

Pick on two axes: how much enterprise SSO/compliance you need, and how much auth UI you want to stop maintaining.

## Key risks

- **User migration.** Password hashes usually can't be exported in plaintext; plan lazy migration (verify against old store on first login, then rehash into the provider) or a bulk hash import if formats are compatible.
- **Lock-in.** Custom claims, rules/actions, and provider-specific tokens are sticky. Keep an abstraction layer at the app boundary so a future swap is bounded.
- **Downtime / session breakage.** A hard cutover invalidates live sessions. Dual-run instead.
- **Cost surprise.** Model real MAU and per-feature add-ons (MFA, enterprise connections) before committing — these are the line items that blow up.

## Rough plan (4 phases, ~4–6 weeks)

1. **Spike & decide (1 wk):** stand up a sandbox tenant, validate SSO + our top 2 login flows, confirm pricing at projected scale.
2. **Integrate behind a flag (1–2 wk):** add the provider for *new* signups; keep existing users on the old path.
3. **Lazy-migrate (2 wk):** on login, validate against the legacy store, then provision the user into the provider. Backfill stragglers in bulk near the end.
4. **Cut over & decommission (1 wk):** route all auth through the provider, monitor error/abandon rates, then retire the legacy auth code and credential store.

## Information needed for a concrete recommendation and cost

I can't name a single provider or put a dollar figure on this until I have the inputs below. Each one changes the answer, so they're grouped by what they decide.

**To choose the provider:**
- **Enterprise SSO timeline** — is SAML/OIDC SSO, SCIM provisioning, or audit-log export needed in the next two quarters, or not on the roadmap? This is the strongest single signal: a near-term yes points to Auth0 or WorkOS; a no opens up Clerk and Cognito.
- **Current stack & hosting** — frontend framework, backend language, and whether we're already committed to AWS. Heavy AWS investment makes Cognito's cost story hard to ignore; a React/Next.js app with little auth UI built makes Clerk's prebuilt components a big time saver.
- **Greenfield vs. existing user base** — are we adding auth, or replacing a system with live users and stored credentials? This sets whether migration risk is even in scope.
- **Data residency / compliance constraints** — any requirement to keep credentials in a specific region or under our own control pushes toward self-host (Keycloak/Ory) despite the operational cost.

**To estimate cost:**
- **Current and projected MAU** (monthly active users) — the primary cost driver for every hosted provider, and the number that makes Auth0 vs. Cognito swing by an order of magnitude at scale.
- **Required paid features** — MFA, enterprise SSO connections (often priced per-connection), passwordless, machine-to-machine tokens. These add-ons, not base MAU, are what usually blow up the bill.
- **Login pattern** — ratio of monthly actives to total accounts, and roughly how often users authenticate. Some providers bill on MAU, others on total users or token volume, so the same user base costs differently per vendor.
- **Tolerance for operational cost** — if self-hosting is on the table, the real comparison is per-user fees vs. the engineering time to run Keycloak/Ory ourselves.

**To size the migration:**
- **Password hash format** (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2) and whether we can read hashes from the current store — this decides bulk import vs. lazy migration, which is the difference between a one-week and a multi-week phase.
- **Existing identity surface** — social logins, magic links, or active third-party integrations that need to be re-established on the new provider.

**Fastest path:** send me current/projected MAU, the SSO timeline, the current auth stack, and the password hash format. With those four I can collapse the shortlist to one provider and produce a real monthly cost estimate at your scale.

## Decision needed

Approve the **spike** (Phase 1) and confirm the provider shortlist. Everything after gates on what the spike shows on pricing-at-scale and SSO fit. To make the spike concrete rather than exploratory, send the four inputs above first.

---

One open judgment call from the gate (it won't auto-pass this for me): **altitude/audience fit.** This is written as an exec/leadership decision memo — recommendation-first, light on implementation detail. If the actual reader is the engineering team who'll build it, I'd add a thin technical layer (the abstraction-boundary interface, token/session specifics, the lazy-migration code path) and trim the framing. Tell me which audience and I'll adjust.

Decision trail: Read the developer's note as a request to expand the existing one-line ask into a full, structured "Information needed" section rather than rewrite the memo, since the rest was already approved-by-silence — kept all prior sections intact and added one section plus two cross-references. Grouped the inputs by decision (provider / cost / migration) over a flat list so each input's purpose is visible. Gated the result with `fable_check` dod_id `doc-planning` → gate PASS (3/3). Not verified / where to look: the D-altitude check is UNCHECKED — whether exec-memo depth matches the real reader is a call only you can make, flagged above.
It's not done yet — it's missing: Asks for audience clarification instead of delivering a final version.. Please give me the complete version.

Done — this is the final version. I committed it to the exec/leadership audience (stated plainly in the header, with a technical design doc to follow the spike) and removed the closing "which audience?" question, since asking it was the defect you flagged. The memo now decides its own altitude instead of deferring that to you. Gate passed: 3/3 deterministic checks (`doc-planning`).

---

# Memo: Migrating User Auth to a Third-Party Provider

**To:** Engineering / Product leadership · **From:** Platform · **Re:** Outsourcing authentication · **Date:** 2026-06-23
**Audience:** Written as an exec/leadership decision memo — recommendation-first, implementation detail kept thin. A separate technical design doc follows once the spike is approved.

## Recommendation

Migrate to a managed identity provider, with **Auth0 (or Clerk for a smaller/greenfield app)** as the default choice. Run it as a phased dual-write migration, not a cutover. The win is removing security-critical code we don't want to own (password storage, MFA, session handling, OAuth flows) and getting SSO/compliance features without building them.

This recommendation is a **default, not a final pick** — it assumes a mid-size B2B/B2C web app with growing enterprise interest. The "Information needed" section below lists what would turn it into a concrete provider choice and a real cost number, and the spike (Phase 1) is where we lock it in.

## Why now

- Auth is high-risk, low-differentiation code. Every hour spent on password reset edge cases or token rotation is an hour not spent on the product.
- Enterprise deals increasingly require SSO (SAML/OIDC), SCIM provisioning, and audit logs — expensive to build, included off the shelf.
- Offloading credential storage shrinks our breach blast radius and simplifies SOC 2 / compliance scope.

## Options considered

| Provider | Best for | Watch-outs |
|---|---|---|
| **Auth0** | Broad feature set, enterprise SSO, flexibility | Pricing scales steeply with MAU; lots of config surface |
| **Clerk** | Fast greenfield, great DX, prebuilt UI | Younger; fewer enterprise/compliance knobs |
| **AWS Cognito** | Already deep in AWS, cost control | Rough DX, painful custom flows |
| **WorkOS** | B2B SSO/SCIM as a bolt-on | Not a full primary-auth replacement |
| **Self-host (Keycloak/Ory)** | Data residency, no per-user cost | We still operate it — defeats much of the point |

Pick on two axes: how much enterprise SSO/compliance you need, and how much auth UI you want to stop maintaining.

## Key risks

- **User migration.** Password hashes usually can't be exported in plaintext; plan lazy migration (verify against old store on first login, then rehash into the provider) or a bulk hash import if formats are compatible.
- **Lock-in.** Custom claims, rules/actions, and provider-specific tokens are sticky. Keep an abstraction layer at the app boundary so a future swap is bounded.
- **Downtime / session breakage.** A hard cutover invalidates live sessions. Dual-run instead.
- **Cost surprise.** Model real MAU and per-feature add-ons (MFA, enterprise connections) before committing — these are the line items that blow up.

## Rough plan (4 phases, ~4–6 weeks)

1. **Spike & decide (1 wk):** stand up a sandbox tenant, validate SSO + our top 2 login flows, confirm pricing at projected scale.
2. **Integrate behind a flag (1–2 wk):** add the provider for *new* signups; keep existing users on the old path.
3. **Lazy-migrate (2 wk):** on login, validate against the legacy store, then provision the user into the provider. Backfill stragglers in bulk near the end.
4. **Cut over & decommission (1 wk):** route all auth through the provider, monitor error/abandon rates, then retire the legacy auth code and credential store.

## Information needed for a concrete recommendation and cost

I can't name a single provider or put a dollar figure on this until I have the inputs below. Each one changes the answer, so they're grouped by what they decide.

**To choose the provider:**
- **Enterprise SSO timeline** — is SAML/OIDC SSO, SCIM provisioning, or audit-log export needed in the next two quarters, or not on the roadmap? This is the strongest single signal: a near-term yes points to Auth0 or WorkOS; a no opens up Clerk and Cognito.
- **Current stack & hosting** — frontend framework, backend language, and whether we're already committed to AWS. Heavy AWS investment makes Cognito's cost story hard to ignore; a React/Next.js app with little auth UI built makes Clerk's prebuilt components a big time saver.
- **Greenfield vs. existing user base** — are we adding auth, or replacing a system with live users and stored credentials? This sets whether migration risk is even in scope.
- **Data residency / compliance constraints** — any requirement to keep credentials in a specific region or under our own control pushes toward self-host (Keycloak/Ory) despite the operational cost.

**To estimate cost:**
- **Current and projected MAU** (monthly active users) — the primary cost driver for every hosted provider, and the number that makes Auth0 vs. Cognito swing by an order of magnitude at scale.
- **Required paid features** — MFA, enterprise SSO connections (often priced per-connection), passwordless, machine-to-machine tokens. These add-ons, not base MAU, are what usually blow up the bill.
- **Login pattern** — ratio of monthly actives to total accounts, and roughly how often users authenticate. Some providers bill on MAU, others on total users or token volume, so the same user base costs differently per vendor.
- **Tolerance for operational cost** — if self-hosting is on the table, the real comparison is per-user fees vs. the engineering time to run Keycloak/Ory ourselves.

**To size the migration:**
- **Password hash format** (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2) and whether we can read hashes from the current store — this decides bulk import vs. lazy migration, which is the difference between a one-week and a multi-week phase.
- **Existing identity surface** — social logins, magic links, or active third-party integrations that need to be re-established on the new provider.

**Fastest path:** current/projected MAU, the SSO timeline, the current auth stack, and the password hash format. With those four, the shortlist collapses to one provider and the spike produces a real monthly cost estimate at our scale. Absent them, the spike defaults to evaluating Auth0 against the mid-size assumption above.

## Decision needed

Approve the **spike** (Phase 1) and confirm the provider shortlist. Everything after gates on what the spike shows on pricing-at-scale and SSO fit. The four inputs above make the spike concrete rather than exploratory; if they aren't ready, the spike proceeds on the default mid-size assumption rather than waiting.

---

Decision trail: Read your feedback as "stop hedging on audience" — so I removed the prior closing note that asked you to pick exec-vs-engineering and instead committed the memo to the exec/leadership default, declaring it in the header and pointing the implementation depth to a follow-on design doc. Kept the "Information needed" section (it's content the reader acts on, not me hedging) but rewrote its tail and the "Decision needed" close so the spike proceeds on a stated default instead of blocking on your inputs. Gated with `fable_check` dod_id `doc-planning` → PASS (3/3). Not verified / where to look: the `D-altitude` item is UNCHECKED by design — the gate can't confirm exec depth is the right call for your actual reader; I made that call rather than asking, so if the real audience is the build team, that's the one line to revisit.
